title: Machine Learning for BI
description: Predictive analytics, supervised learning, and classification algorithms.

sections.title: Introduction to Predictive Analytics
sections.lesson:
Introduction to Predictive Modeling

Predictive modeling is one of the core components of modern analytics. It allows organizations to move beyond understanding what happened, and instead forecast what is likely to happen in the future. This module introduces the foundations, process, and business applications of predictive modeling.

What is Predictive Modeling?

Predictive Modeling is a statistical and machine learning approach used to analyze historical data and predict future outcomes.

It identifies patterns in past data and uses those patterns to estimate future events or behaviors.

Key idea: Predictive modeling answers the question — “What is likely to happen next?”

From Descriptive to Predictive Analytics

Type of Analytics Question Answered Example
Descriptive What happened? Monthly sales report
Diagnostic Why did it happen? Sales dropped due to low inventory
Predictive What will happen? Forecast next month’s sales
Prescriptive What should we do? Increase marketing budget by 15%

Types of Predictive Models

1. Regression (Predicting Numbers)
  • Sales forecasting
  • Price prediction
  • Demand estimation
2. Classification (Predicting Categories)
  • Customer churn (Yes/No)
  • Loan approval (Approve/Reject)
  • Spam detection
3. Time Series Forecasting
  • Enrollment forecasting
  • Monthly revenue prediction
Key idea: The type of model depends on whether the output is a number, a category, or time-based data.

The Predictive Modeling Process

  1. Define the Problem
  2. Collect Historical Data
  3. Clean and Prepare Data
  4. Split Data (Training and Testing)
  5. Train the Model
  6. Evaluate the Model
  7. Deploy and Monitor
Important: A model must perform well on new data, not just on the training data.

Simple Concept Example (Linear Regression)

Suppose we want to predict sales based on advertising budget:


sales = 5000 + (20 * advertising_budget)

If advertising_budget = 1000 Predicted sales = 5000 + (20 × 1000) = 25,000

How Do We Evaluate a Model?

Different metrics are used depending on the model type:

  • Accuracy – Overall correctness (Classification)
  • Precision & Recall – Quality of positive predictions
  • RMSE – Measures prediction error (Regression)
  • R² – Explains variance in regression
Tip: Always compare your model with a simple baseline prediction.

Business Applications of Predictive Modeling

  • Customer churn prediction
  • Fraud detection
  • Inventory demand forecasting
  • University enrollment forecasting
  • Agricultural yield prediction
Key idea: Predictive modeling transforms BI dashboards into forward-looking decision tools.

Quick Reflection Questions

  1. What is the difference between regression and classification?
  2. Why do we split data into training and testing sets?
  3. Give one example of predictive modeling in an educational institution.

sections.title: Data Normalization for ML
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Understand the role of preprocessing in machine learning
  • Identify key preprocessing techniques in Orange
  • Apply preprocessing steps such as normalization and imputation
  • Analyze the effect of preprocessing on model performance

What is Data Preprocessing?

Data preprocessing is the process of preparing raw data before applying machine learning algorithms. In Orange, this is done using the Preprocess widget.

Preprocessing improves data quality, making models more accurate and reliable.

Handling Missing Data (Imputation)

Missing values are common in datasets. Imputation replaces missing values with estimated ones.

  • Mean – average (numerical)
  • Median – middle value
  • Mode – most frequent (categorical)
Always handle missing data BEFORE applying other preprocessing steps.
Example:
[5, ?, 15] → Mean = 10 → [5, 10, 15]

Normalization (Scaling Data)

Normalization adjusts values to a common scale so that no feature dominates others.

Common Methods:
  • Min-Max Scaling (0 to 1)
  • Z-score (mean = 0, std = 1)
Formula (Min-Max):
(x - min) / (max - min)
Useful when features have different units (e.g., age vs salary).
Example:
[10, 20, 30] → 20 becomes 0.5

Discretization

Discretization converts continuous values into categories.

Example:
Age → Young | Middle | Old
Helps simplify models and improve interpretability.

Feature Selection

Removes irrelevant or redundant features to improve performance.

  • Remove constant features
  • Remove noisy data
Keeping useless features can reduce model accuracy.

Typical Orange Workflow

File → Preprocess → Model → Test & Score
Preprocess is always placed BEFORE modeling.

Reflection Questions

  1. Why is preprocessing important before training a model?
  2. What happens if you skip normalization?
  3. When should you use discretization instead of normalization?
  4. How does removing features affect model performance?

sections.title: Data Imputation
sections.lesson:
What is the purpose of Orange?

Think about how machine learning is usually done. Most tools require coding. Orange is different.

🤔 How do you think a beginner would benefit from a drag-and-drop ML tool?
Orange allows users to build machine learning workflows visually using widgets. This removes the need for heavy coding and helps beginners focus on concepts.

What is Imputation?

Real-world datasets are messy. Sometimes values are missing.

🤔 If a dataset has missing values, what do you think will happen during training?
Imputation is the process of replacing missing values with estimated ones. Without it, many machine learning algorithms will fail or perform poorly.

Why use Mean or Most Frequent?

One simple way to fill missing data is by using averages or common values.

🤔 When would using the average be a good idea?
Mean is useful for numerical data because it represents a central value. Most frequent works for categorical data like labels or categories.

What is Model-Based Imputation?

Instead of guessing, we can predict missing values using other data.

🤔 How is predicting missing data better than using averages?
Model-based imputation uses algorithms (like k-NN) to predict values, making it more accurate because it considers relationships in data.

What happens if data is not cleaned?

Machine learning models rely heavily on data quality.

🤔 What do you think happens if we train a model with messy data?
Poor data leads to poor predictions. This is known as "Garbage in, garbage out."

When should we remove rows?

Sometimes instead of filling missing values, we delete them.

🤔 Is it always safe to remove data?
Removing rows is useful when only a few records are missing values. But removing too much data can reduce dataset quality and size.

Why avoid imputing class labels?

The class label is what the model tries to predict.

🤔 What happens if we "guess" the correct answer during training?
Imputing class labels can introduce bias and make the model learn incorrect patterns.

What is the risk of improper preprocessing?

Preprocessing must be done carefully, especially during validation.

🤔 What is data leakage and why is it dangerous?
Data leakage happens when information from test data is used during training. This leads to overly optimistic and misleading results.

How does 1-Nearest Neighbor (1-NN) help in imputation?

Orange uses 1-NN as a default for model-based imputation.

🤔 Why would using a nearby data point be useful to fill missing values?
1-NN finds the closest record to the missing value and uses its value to fill in, maintaining consistency in the dataset.

What is the difference between numeric and categorical imputation?

Not all data types can be treated the same way.

🤔 How would you handle missing numbers vs missing categories differently?
Numeric data can use mean or median, while categorical data often uses the most frequent value. Using the wrong method can distort the data.

Why is data preprocessing considered more important than algorithms?

A model is only as good as the data it sees.

🤔 Can a complex algorithm fix poor-quality data?
Even the best algorithm fails if data is messy. Preprocessing ensures the algorithm receives clean and meaningful inputs.

When should random imputation be used?

Sometimes we want to maintain variability.

🤔 What might happen if we always use the same value to fill missing data?
Random imputation preserves variability and prevents artificially uniform data, which may improve model robustness.

How does imputation affect model evaluation?

Filling missing values impacts the final metrics.

🤔 Why might an improper imputation give misleading accuracy scores?
If the imputation introduces bias or uses information from test data, metrics like accuracy may appear better than they really are, leading to data leakage.

Why is normalization or scaling important after imputation?

Imputation fixes missing values, but features may still differ in scale.

🤔 How could differences in feature scales affect model performance?
Features on different scales can dominate model training, e.g., larger numbers overpower smaller ones. Normalization ensures all features contribute fairly.

What is the final takeaway about missing data handling in Orange?

Handling missing data correctly is critical for real-world applications.

🤔 Think about what you would do if you encountered a dataset with 20% missing values.
Proper imputation or removal strategies are essential. Choose methods based on data type, missing value proportion, and downstream tasks. Orange provides flexible tools to experiment and visualize the impact.

🧠 Final Reflection

  • Which imputation method would you trust most? Why?
  • When is deleting data better than filling it?
  • How can poor preprocessing affect real-world systems?

sections.title: Feature Selection
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Understand the concept and purpose of feature selection
  • Identify benefits and risks of selecting features
  • Use Orange tools (Rank widget) for feature selection
  • Analyze the impact of features on model performance

Concept Explanation

Feature selection is the process of choosing the most relevant variables (features) from a dataset to use in building a predictive model. Instead of using all available data, we focus only on the features that significantly contribute to predicting the target variable.

Including irrelevant or redundant features can introduce noise, reduce model accuracy, and increase computational cost. By selecting only the important features, we can build simpler, faster, and more accurate models.

Key Concepts from the Quiz

  • Goal: Select the most relevant variables for prediction
  • Benefits: Improved accuracy, faster computation, simpler models
  • Risks: Losing important information if too many features are removed
  • Common Issue: Too many irrelevant features reduce model performance

Feature Selection in Orange

Typical workflow:


File → Impute → Rank → Select Columns → Model
  • File: Load dataset
  • Impute: Handle missing values
  • Rank: Evaluate feature importance
  • Select Columns: Keep only important features

Example Scenario

A school wants to predict student performance. The dataset includes:

  • Study hours
  • Attendance
  • Favorite color
  • Previous grades

Using feature selection, "Favorite color" may be removed because it does not affect academic performance, while "Study hours" and "Previous grades" are retained.

Important Notes

  • Do not remove features blindly—always analyze importance
  • Too many features → Overfitting risk
  • Too few features → Underfitting risk

Reflection Questions

  1. Why is feature selection important in predictive analytics?
  2. What might happen if irrelevant features are included?
  3. How does the Rank widget help in decision-making?
  4. Can removing too many features harm the model? Explain.
  5. Give a real-life example where feature selection is useful.

Student Checklist

  • I understand what feature selection is
  • I can identify relevant vs irrelevant features
  • I can use the Rank widget in Orange
  • I understand the risks of overfitting and underfitting

sections.title: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Understand the concept of kNN algorithm
  • Configure kNN widget in Orange Data Mining
  • Interpret distance metrics and weights
  • Apply kNN in real-world scenarios

What is kNN?

The k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) algorithm is a lazy learning method that predicts outcomes based on the closest data points in the dataset.

In simple terms: "Your output is influenced by your nearest neighbors."

How kNN Works in Orange

  1. Select number of neighbors (k)
  2. Choose a distance metric
  3. Set weighting method
  4. Compute nearest instances
  5. Predict based on majority vote or average

Example Workflow:
File → Preprocess → kNN → Test & Score → Predictions

Key Parameters of kNN Widget

1. Number of Neighbors (k)

Controls how many nearby instances influence prediction.

2. Distance Metrics
  • Euclidean Distance
  • Manhattan Distance
  • Mahalanobis Distance
3. Weights
  • Uniform: equal influence
  • Distance-based: closer neighbors matter more

Real-World Example

Student Performance Prediction

  • Inputs: study hours, attendance, quiz scores
  • kNN finds similar students
  • Prediction based on majority outcome

Key Insight

kNN does not build a model in advance. Instead, it memorizes the dataset and compares new inputs to existing examples.

Understanding “k” in kNN

In kNN, the value k determines how many nearby data points are considered when making a prediction. Instead of relying on a single neighbor, the algorithm looks at multiple neighbors to make a more balanced decision.

The main idea behind kNN is simple: data points that are close to each other are likely to be similar. When a new input is given, the algorithm finds the closest known data points and uses them to predict the result.

Distance Metrics Matter

kNN relies on distance to determine similarity. The most commonly used metric is Euclidean distance, which measures straight-line distance between two points.

When k is very small (e.g., k = 1), the model becomes highly sensitive to individual data points. This may lead to overfitting, where the model performs well on training data but poorly on new data.

Importance of Normalization

Features must be scaled properly before applying kNN. Without normalization, features with larger values can dominate distance calculations, leading to incorrect results.

Not all neighbors need to contribute equally. In distance-based weighting, closer neighbors have more influence on the prediction than those farther away.

Type of Learning in kNN

kNN is a lazy learning algorithm. It does not build a model in advance but instead waits until a prediction is needed before computing results.

When k is too large, the model becomes too generalized and may ignore important patterns. This leads to underfitting.

How kNN Makes Predictions

kNN does not train a neural network or build complex models. Instead, it: computes distances, finds nearest neighbors, and performs majority voting (classification) or averaging (regression).

In classification, kNN predicts the most common class among neighbors. In regression, it predicts the average value of the neighbors.

Summary

  • kNN is based on similarity and distance
  • Choice of k affects performance
  • Normalization is critical
  • Used for both classification and regression
  • kNN is a lazy learning algorithm
  • Uses distance-based similarity
  • Simple but powerful for classification and regression

sections.title: Logistic Regression
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Understand Logistic Regression in a simple way
  • Explain the role of the sigmoid function
  • Apply Logistic Regression in real-world scenarios
  • Build and interpret a model using Orange

Concept Explanation

Logistic Regression is used to answer Yes/No questions by estimating probability. Instead of directly saying "Yes" or "No", it first calculates how likely the outcome is.

Main Idea: Score → Probability → Decision

Sigmoid Function (The Core of Logistic Regression)

The sigmoid function is what makes Logistic Regression special. It transforms any value into a number between 0 and 1. 

p = 1 / (1 + e^-z)

Why is this important?

  • Ensures outputs are valid probabilities
  • Smoothly converts low scores to low probability
  • Converts high scores to high probability
Think of sigmoid as a "probability converter".

Behavior of sigmoid:

  • Very negative score → close to 0
  • Score near 0 → around 0.5
  • Very positive score → close to 1

Real-World Examples

1. Student Passing Prediction

A teacher wants to predict if a student will pass.

  • Inputs: Study Hours, Attendance
  • Output: Probability of passing

Example results:

  • 0.85 → Pass
  • 0.40 → Fail
2. Loan Approval (Banking)

A bank decides whether to approve a loan.

  • Inputs: Income, Debt, Credit Score
  • Output: Probability of repayment
  • 0.90 → Approve
  • 0.25 → Reject
3. Email Spam Detection

Email systems classify messages as spam or not.

  • Inputs: Keywords, sender, frequency
  • Output: Probability of being spam
  • 0.95 → Spam
  • 0.10 → Not Spam

Orange Workflow (Step-by-Step)

  1. File → Load dataset
  2. Logistic Regression → Build model
  3. Test & Score → Evaluate accuracy
  4. Predictions → View results
No coding needed—just connect widgets visually.

Key Notes

  • Used for binary classification
  • Outputs probability (not exact values)
  • Sigmoid ensures results stay between 0 and 1
  • Threshold converts probability to decision

Reflection Questions

  • Why is sigmoid important in Logistic Regression?
  • What happens if the threshold changes from 0.5 to 0.7?
  • Can you think of another Yes/No problem?

sections.title: Decision Trees
sections.lesson:
Learning Objectives

  • Understand how the Tree widget builds decision tree models
  • Explain how Tree Viewer visualizes classification and regression trees
  • Identify key parameters affecting tree construction
  • Analyze model outputs using interactive visualization

Concept Explanation

1. Tree Widget (Model)

The Tree widget is used to create a decision tree model. It splits data into branches based on feature values to improve prediction accuracy.

A decision tree divides data based on class purity using:
  • Information Gain (classification)
  • Mean Squared Error (regression)

It supports both classification and regression tasks and produces:

  • Learner – the algorithm
  • Model – the trained decision tree
Key Parameters:
  • Binary tree splitting
  • Minimum instances per leaf
  • Maximum tree depth
  • Stopping criteria (majority threshold)

2. Tree Viewer Widget (Visualization)

The Tree Viewer displays the generated decision tree in a 2D visual format.

It allows users to interactively explore the model by clicking nodes to inspect data.
Key Features:
  • Zoom in/out and adjust tree depth
  • Node selection to extract subsets of data
  • Color nodes based on class or statistical values
  • Adjust edge width based on data proportions

Selecting a node outputs the corresponding dataset, enabling exploratory data analysis.

Workflow Example


File → Tree → Tree Viewer → Data Table / Scatter Plot
  • Load dataset using File widget
  • Connect to Tree to build model
  • Visualize using Tree Viewer
  • Select nodes to analyze subsets

Key Insights

  • Tree builds the model, Tree Viewer explains it
  • Tree depth controls complexity (overfitting vs simplicity)
  • Visualization helps interpret model decisions
  • Node selection enables interactive data exploration

Reflection Questions

  1. Why is controlling tree depth important?
  2. How does Tree Viewer help in understanding predictions?
  3. What happens if the tree is too deep?
  4. How can node selection support decision-making?
